problem_summary: 2017 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy type): design governmental and welfare policies for "Population Zero," an initial colony of 10,000 humans on Mars (project "UTOPIA: 2100"), to build an egalitarian society that maximizes both economic productivity and social welfare (income, education, gender equality) over 10- and 100-year horizons, including recommending the initial population's demographics, optimal salary/minimum-wage distribution, and emergency-migration capacity. This team won an Outstanding Award by building a full agent-based socioeconomic simulation in NetLogo, driven entirely by real US Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) and Census data, and by inventing three custom, computationally cheap policy metrics (Income I, Education E, Equality Q) that they could optimize against by sweeping policy levers.
models_used: Agent-based model (ABM) in NetLogo: ~10,000 'turtle' agents, each a Martian citizen with attributes (age, sex, education, job, income, percentile, lifestage 0-4, innovator flag, num-babies, death age) progressing through a born->school->college->job-search->work->retire->death lifecycle, ticked 6 times/year

Discrete-time job-search / education Markov-style decision process: on each job search an agent who fails either keeps searching (unemployed) with prob p2/p4/p6 or returns to school to the next degree with prob (1-p); p4=46515/(46515+18683+6987)=0.49 etc. derived directly from 2015 Census educational-attainment counts; includes an 'underemployed' state (overqualified agents keep searching while working)

Data-extrapolation submodel: exponential fit on 5 years (2011-2015) of BLS data across 22 occupation categories to project per-occupation income and worker counts to year 2100; job availability driven by normalized job ratios v_i / sum(v_i) times the live job-market size, refreshed once per year (not per tick) to model labor-market inflexibility

Income-percentile salary model: each hire assigned a percentile (10-50 at start); income linearly interpolated between known BLS percentile incomes, e.g. (25-x)*P10 + (x-10)*P25; floor = minimum wage below P10, cap = P90; percentile grows ~4.65/yr (target +20 percentiles over tenure)

Stochastic demographic submodels: birth probability p_baby = k*((a/6-3)/((a/6-4)^2+1))/(b+1) peaking at age 26, calibrated by trial-and-error k=0.265 to hit world birth rate 2.4; lifespan drawn from a gamma distribution (mean 100, sd 10); 2%/yr job-loss hazard; gender-and-education-dependent stay-at-home-parent probabilities

Macroeconomic indicator submodels: progressive 5-bracket tax system keyed to minimum wage / income mean / 75th / 90th percentiles with rates OTR/6...OTR/2; GDP = consumer expenditure (Income*MPC + Welfare, MPC=0.9) + government spending + investment (savings/(1+inflation)) + net exports(=0); government debt = cumulative deficit; dual-sinusoid inflation model Inflation(t)=0.0025 sin(2pi t/20)+0.008-0.0005 cos(2pi t) (20-yr business cycle + annual fluctuation), modeled on Canada's stable ~1.3% rate

Three custom optimization metrics: Income I = mu_I * sigma_I where sigma_I = sigma/(sigma^2+0.55) penalizes both too-large and too-small wage gaps (0.55 tuned so the penalty peaks at sigma=0.74, back-derived from a target Gini 0.4 via G=erf(sigma/2)); Education E = Unemployment * Students/(sum of faculty years-of-education), lower is better; Equality Q = (1/LaborForce) * sum_j c_j*W_j comparing female occupational share Mars vs Earth

Parameter-sweep / sensitivity analysis used as the optimization engine: swept minimum wage (65k-80k), overall tax rate (30-90%), maternity/paternity leave length, and 6 initial-population compositions plus migration sizes (10k-100k) to read off optimal policy from the metric responses
key_techniques: Justified the model-class choice explicitly: argued ABM over differential-equation models because ODEs cannot capture 'personal initiative' (individual incentive) or discrete agent interactions, and tied this to a real-world claim (lack of personal initiative as a cause of communism's downfall) — turning a method choice into a narrative argument

Grounded every parameter in cited real data (BLS 22 occupation categories with full percentile income tables, Census educational-attainment counts, UN life-expectancy projections, Canadian inflation, US MPC) rather than invented numbers — 56+ references

Invented cheap surrogate metrics for expensive quantities: explicitly noted the Gini coefficient is O(n^2) and built I/sigma_I as an O(n) computational alternative, then calibrated its constant to match a real Gini target via the log-normal G=erf(sigma/2) identity

Used the dual-sinusoid inflation fit as a clean closed-form way to capture both the ~20-year business cycle and annual jitter from a noisy historical CPI series, after explicitly rejecting direct extrapolation of erratic world inflation in favor of stable Canadian data
Multi-timescale modeling discipline: ticks 6x/year for agent dynamics, jobs reset annually for labor inflexibility, metrics evaluated every 2 months — each cadence justified by a real-world mechanism

Discovered and fixed an emergent artifact: a static 'baby-constant' caused an initial baby boom that flooded the job market ~20 years later (unemployment spike); they ramped the baby-constant linearly to its final value over 20 years, which both smoothed the wave and matched the real behavior that migrants settle gradually
Framed unemployment as the single coupling variable linking all three metrics (raises E, lowers I, distorts Q), giving the judges one clean causal lever to reason about the whole system

Sensitivity analysis doubled as the optimization method — instead of a separate optimizer, they swept each policy lever and read the optimum off the metric tables (min wage $70k, leave lengths, tax-rate vs debt-vs-investment tradeoff)
why_it_won:
"For a deliberately open-ended, almost sci-fi policy prompt, this team delivered a genuinely runnable, data-grounded simulation rather than hand-waving. The win factors a judge would mark: (1) Correct model-class selection with an articulate justification (ABM captures individual incentive and discrete interactions that ODEs cannot). (2) Real data everywhere — BLS occupational percentiles and Census attainment counts feed actual transition probabilities and salary distributions, so the 'Mars' numbers are anchored to Earth reality and extrapolated by fitted growth rates. (3) Self-aware engineering of metrics — naming the Gini coefficient as the gold standard, identifying its O(n^2) cost, and constructing a calibrated O(n) surrogate shows judges the team understood both the economics and the computation. (4) The three bespoke metrics (I, E, Q) map one-to-one onto the prompt's three required factors (income, education, equality) and are directly optimizable, which makes the policy recommendations fall out of the model rather than being asserted. (5) Strong coverage of every sub-question: demographics (mean age 37, 1:10 innovator:producer, 50:50 sex), salary distribution, minimum wage $70k, leave lengths, the 100-year sensitivity run, and the emergency-migration scenario (10k-100k). (6) The emergent baby-boom artifact and its fix demonstrate the model was actually run, observed, and debugged — high-credibility evidence. (7) Outstanding-level packaging: a policy memo as letter to a fictional director, a vivid 'life of Bob and Alice' narrative walkthrough, a lifecycle flowchart, and an explicit strengths/weaknesses section."
- key_techniques
section_highlights: "Summary sheet leads with concrete, quantified policy answers (mean age 37, 1:10 innovators, $70k minimum wage, 10mo/3mo leave) rather than vague claims. A two-page policy-memo cover letter addressed to a fictional 'Director of LIFE' translates the math into executive-readable recommendations — exactly the register a policy problem rewards. The model is explained through a narrative device, the 'life of Bob' (job search, going back to school, underemployment) and 'Alice' (childbirth, maternity then paternity leave, retirement, death), which makes a complex ABM legible, supported by a lifecycle flowchart (Figure 1). Definitions/Assumptions section lists each assumption with an immediate one-line justification. Each metric (I, E, Q) is presented with its formula, an Earth baseline value for calibration (e.g., US income metric ~88,384; target Mars I>150,000; E<0.03; Q>3.5), and a goal threshold. Sensitivity analysis is a clean comparative table over 6 population compositions plus the migration-size sweep, and the Strengths/Weaknesses section is honest (admits data-dependence over 80 years, purely economic scope ignoring physical infrastructure, stochastic variance requiring multiple runs, no family structure)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Several key constants are tuned by trial-and-error rather than principled fit (birth constant k=0.265; the income-metric constant 0.55 is back-derived through a chain of approximations: assumed log-normal incomes + assumed target Gini 0.4 + G=erf(sigma/2)), so the headline metric's shape rests on stacked assumptions

The model is purely economic and explicitly ignores physical infrastructure (housing, food, life support) — a glaring omission for a Mars-colonization prompt; they acknowledge it but it undercuts claims like 'can support far more than 10,000'

All 'Mars' behavior is just present-day US/Canada data extrapolated by constant exponential growth rates over ~80 years; the assumption that incomes, worker counts, and prices all grow at today's rates is fragile and, as they admit, small data errors compound massively over 80 years

Stochastic single-run reporting: many headline numbers (e.g., min-wage and leave-length tables) come from runs with no reported confidence intervals or replication counts despite the model being probabilistic; the maternity/paternity table is so noisy the team itself calls the relationship 'heavily arbitrary'

Internal inconsistency/typos in reported results: the unemployment 'rate' formula is written as Workers/LaborForce (which is employment, not unemployment); income-metric magnitudes differ between the results body (~180k) and sensitivity section (>400k) without reconciliation; lifestage list is described as '0,1,2,3, and 4' (five stages mislabeled as four)

The Equality metric Q's stated goal (Q>3.5) versus reported values (~10) and the units/definition (sum of squared share differences weighted by women count, divided by labor force) are not clearly dimensionally tied to the 'goal', making Q the weakest-specified of the three metrics

The raw NetLogo source dumped into the appendix contains visible TODOs ('TO DO: FAMILIES', unset minimum-wage/tax values) revealing the code was not fully finished, and some equations as transcribed are ambiguous
reusable_patterns: For open-ended 'design a society/policy' prompts, build an agent-based model and justify ABM-vs-ODE explicitly in terms of what individual heterogeneity and discrete interactions capture that aggregate equations cannot

Anchor a speculative/future scenario to real present-day datasets (here BLS occupation percentiles + Census attainment) and project forward with fitted growth rates — derive transition probabilities and distributions directly from data counts rather than guessing

Invent one custom, normalized, computationally cheap metric per dimension the prompt asks you to optimize, then calibrate each metric's free constant against a real-world benchmark (e.g., tune a constant so your surrogate peaks at the sigma implied by a target Gini) and report an Earth baseline + a target threshold for each
When the gold-standard quantity is expensive (Gini, O(n^2)), name it, state the cost, then build and justify an O(n) surrogate — judges reward this awareness

Use parameter sweeps as the optimization engine: vary each policy lever (min wage, tax rate, leave length, population mix) and read the optimum off metric-response tables, and present the tradeoffs (e.g., tax rate vs government debt vs innovator investment) rather than a single point answer
Identify the one variable that couples all your metrics (here, unemployment) and use it as the narrative spine connecting the submodels
Explain a complex model through a concrete named-agent narrative ('the life of Bob/Alice') plus a lifecycle flowchart to make it legible to judges quickly

Actually run the model long enough to surface emergent artifacts (the 20-year-delayed baby boom), then add a mechanism to fix it (ramp the baby-constant) and report both — this is strong evidence of a working model

Fit noisy cyclic historical series (inflation) with a sum of sinusoids capturing distinct timescales (business cycle + annual) instead of naive extrapolation, and pick a more stable proxy dataset when the primary series is too erratic
Package policy results as an executive memo/letter to the fictional stakeholder, leading with quantified recommendations, and include an honest strengths/weaknesses section
